# portcourt — CLI reference

A general C→Rust port evidence court. All paths in the config resolve relative to the config file; the config defaults to `./portcourt.toml`.

| command | summary |
|---|---|
| `portcourt check [config.toml]` | the gate — verify every module's claim against the evidence |
| `portcourt explain <fn> [config.toml]` | the port status of one function |
| `portcourt next [file] [config.toml]` | the porting driver — which functions are still missing |
| `portcourt report [config.toml]` | the parity table across all modules |

## `portcourt check [config.toml]`

the gate — verify every module's claim against the evidence

Closure math: for each `[module]` in the config, a `claim = "complete"` module must have zero missing functions and zero doc-only false hits on compiled functions; a `min_parity` sets a floor on active parity; and every court in `requires` must be sealed in the claim ladder. Any over-claim prints `✗ …` and the process exits non-zero, so `check` is a build gate. Modules present in the parity view but undeclared in the config are surfaced as a note, never silently ignored.

## `portcourt explain <fn> [config.toml]`

the port status of one function

Locates `<fn>` across every module in the parity view and reports whether it is PORTED, MISSING (no Rust `fn`), DOC-ONLY (a name that appears only in a comment — a false hit, not a port), or DISABLED (`#if 0` in the C, not a port target), along with the Rust module that ports it and any court whose `proven` prose names it.

## `portcourt next [file] [config.toml]`

the porting driver — which functions are still missing

Lists, per module (or for one `file`), the functions whose status is `missing` (no port yet) and `doc_only` (a comment to reword). This is the worklist a port drives off of.

## `portcourt report [config.toml]`

the parity table across all modules

Prints active parity %, ported / missing / doc-only / disabled counts per module and a grand total — the whole port at a glance.
